speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 13, 1995. The survivor that we are interviewing is Simon Stemer. The interviewer is Lenore Bienenfeld Weinstein. The interview is taking place in Miami Beach, Florida, the United States of America, and the language is English. My name is Lenore Bienenfeld Weinstein. Today is November 13, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Simon Stemer. The interview will be in English, and it is in Miami Beach, Florida. Can you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Simon Stemer-- S-T-E-M-E-R-- Simon-- S-I-M-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given any other names at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No other names.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jesiah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jesiah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is the 17 of January. I was born 1903.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that makes you how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that will be-- in January, I will be 87 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell me, where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in a small town in Poland. The name of the town was Krasnystaw. I live there till the time when the war-- till 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell me the name of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Alukim. My mother's name was Figa Dysze, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and do you know the names of any of your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] The grandmother-- I know one grandmother, and I don't know the other grandpa. And I don't remember the other grandma's name. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two brothers, two sisters. One brother was married and lived in Krasnystaw. He have six children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name was Hersch. And my brother, Mikhael got married [NON-ENGLISH], And he have two kids. And me and my two sisters-- we were not married till the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your sisters' names? Your sister's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister was Hanna, and one sister was Elcie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what life was like in your town before the war, what your daily activities were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- in Europe, the living was very hard. Only my mother have a small grocery store. And the kids helped out in the store. And we make a living. When I was young, I was belonging to different organizations in Krasnystaw-- the designers organization, the [NON-ENGLISH] organization. I was the president of [NON-ENGLISH]. We did what we can do for Israel. We collect money for Israel, and only is first not that big possibility-- this life in Poland for the young people. When Poland is-- was-- in 1918, in November 8, when they announced Poland is-- is back-- be a Polish country, the constitution said, everybody who is living in the Polish territory have the right of the citizen-- Jews, Polacks, Russians, Ukrainian-- everybody have the same right. Only like it showed that a couple years later, in the '20, they start to look-- they start-- started anti-Semitism. And in the the town, Lemberg, there was arrested one man, Stanislaw Steiger. For they was telling-- he killed-- he want to kill the Polish president. Only there was the court, and he was out from the court. Anyway, the anti-Semitism was very hard. The Polish people, they try to-- to get out the possibility for the Jewish, that live in Poland, to exist. What they was be able to do, they did-- they put them in high-- high taxes. And it-- and it-- for us, it was possible just to cut the Jewish profits they was doing. Like in the last years, like, we have in the grocery, we have Polish customers, we have Jewish customers. Like before Christmas, they used to-- in our town was a college, two Polish students stand up by the door. This-- and-- and this was in the last years-- and the Polish people was ashamed to go in, in this thing. It was very hard to live. Even--it was a lot to do. Like, the people in-- in Warsaw, they have the colleges. They make the left side for the Jews, and the right side for the Pole, to be separated. And this was a lot-- a lot of time, they was attacking the Jews. The Jew don't have even the right to enter something-- if he enters something, he was arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, I was walking-- it was in the night-- with my friend. It was about 10 o'clock. And two-- two Polish guys was hitting us. We started to call police. Police come to, come and-- and he said, what are you doing so late, the Jews in the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: They was-- even the government, was always against the Jews. Even-- the Jewish people won't emigrate from Poland. Only it was not a possibility. They want to go to Israel. And Israel was the Englishmen, that they don't give too many certificates. They were-- they went to Belgium-- not legal. Some people, they went to French-- not legal. Some people, they went to United States. Only, not too many people left Poland. Maybe, like if Poland was 3.5 million Jews, maybe about 200,000 in all the years, they left the country. It was not a possibility to-- to go out of this country. It was very hard. The-- Even--the last year, it was the senate was over there, a senator, a lady, Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH]-- she-- she make a statement-- the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. There's too much pain for the animals, like the Jews are killing the animals. They have to-- to discontinue this do. And that was already signed by the senate for the 1st of September, 1939. They have to be stopped, the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. And this life was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and this was before the war happened? There were all these anti-Semitic acts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, Friday, September 1, 1939, 6 o'clock in the morning, the radio announced the German start the war on Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, before we get to the start of the war, I'd like to ask you a few more questions about what it was like before the war. Was your family observant? Were they religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was very religious. They was very orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they attend a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- even they attend a synagogue, and my mother was very orthodox. I even went over there every day to shul, two times a day. My family was a very orthodox family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell me what the holidays were like, such as Rosh Hashanah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the holiday-- the celebration-- the celebration for the holidays was very nice. Only, like, we have a grocery store. It was not allowed Sunday to be in the store, to open. Even it's-- even it was coming out that said, [NON-ENGLISH], like Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, even like a Saturday, Sunday, [NON-ENGLISH], Monday, Tuesday, [NON-ENGLISH]. Was very hard for the Jews to make a living. For the-- there was a lot of people, where they was-- they-- they have trades, like tailors or shoemakers. If they don't work those four days, it was very hard to make a living. Celebration-- the temples, they was packed. Every holiday was packed at celebrations. And people was living-- in every town was some rich people-- only a small percentage, a very small percentage. The most people was working people. They don't took in too many people with government jobs-- just like tailors, or-- or carpenters, or shoemakers. They was working, and they make a living. And the storekeepers, they was making a living. Only it was very, very poor country for the-- very poor country.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was your town that you grew up with? How many people were in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in our town, was about 500 Jewish families. The town had about 8,000 Polish people. There was about 500 Jewish families. It was a small town. We have nice temples. We have a rabbi. And like before-- it comes like-- they have-- the Polish people have celebration the 3rd of May. It is the Jewish have come together to the temple. And the rabbi have a speech for the government try-- the government is good. They send all over-- the government people to the temples. In his speeches, the rabbi said, in Hebrew-- [SPEAKING HEBREW]. This means you have to pray for the good guy and the-- the government. And he was talking about a government-- how the government is good for the Jews. In our town, was the rabbi, he said, o, this man, what he is staying here-- he is [SPEAKING HEBREW]. He love Jews. He have to say it. He-- you know, he don't love Jews, only he had to say it. This what-- this was the Polish government. If not, this was a lot of Jewish organizations. This was the Zionist organizations, this was [NON-ENGLISH] organizations, this was the [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]-- this was a lot of young people. Only, everybody was thinking to go to Israel, only the doors was locked. This was a very hard life for the Jewish people in-- in that town-- in that-- in Polish. And every town like this. Like this was a-- a small town. [NON-ENGLISH]. Before the war, they come out with the pogrom. They-- they was-- they started to hit all over the Jews in this [NON-ENGLISH]. And they was arrested, some Jews, where they was standing, again. It was very, very difficult to live over there. Only there was no place to go. You have to-- you have to take it, what has come. The young people, they just was hoping to go to Israel. It was their only hope. Only this was not a possibility. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just went to cheder. And-- and when my brother got married, even before I was helping in the store, because my brother got married in '26, '25, I was helping in the store, [INAUDIBLE]. And the other brother was working. He was a painter. And the older brother got married, and he have a lumber business. It was not a big success-- only he make a living. It was very hard-- only make a living. And my sister-- it was very hard to give the kids an education. I have one sister-- Hanna-- she want to be educated. And my mother have not the possibility to give her the education. She finished grammar school-- this is seventh grade. And she took a job for-- helping bookkeeping. In the night, she went to college, and she got a degree from the college. Until the war broke out, she was a bookkeeper in [NON-ENGLISH]. My younger sister, she was making brassieres-- corset brassieres. And then this was the life where-- in-- in our family. The cousins-- they make a living only. It was very hard to live. Like, I have an uncle, this uncle what you see here. His son, in 1922, he left to Israel-- to America, to United States. Is-- the family was very orthodox. My-- my uncle went, the usual day, was going to the rabbi's, like they go here. They went to the [NON-ENGLISH] a rabbi. They're telling the story. My uncle went to a rabbi, and the rabbi said he should sit shiva, liked the son is that he went in a such a country, for it is not orthodox, as [INAUDIBLE]. Only my uncle was in the wheat business-- our family was in-- my father have a wheat business, too. He was making a living. In the last years, in the '30s, he was not making-- be able to make a living. They got a son in Chicago. They wrote the whole story, how this life is. And he start to send them money every month. And he was living from this money. He was out of work. He was closed up the business. And he was living with this money, what the son sent him. It's the-- we was always talking-- before, the rabbi said, he have to sit shiva only when he needed, then he didn't have to live. He didn't have money to exist. He called his son, and he was a good son. He sent him every month, I don't know how many dollars. Only he lived from this life. And this son, what he lived in Chicago, he had a candy store. He helped out. His one sister got married-- he helped them out. And the other sister got married-- he helped-- he sent him some money to be-- exist in some kind of a business. This was very-- very bad living in-- in Poland for the Jews. You see--I say it not once, it's-- I am proud that I am here in United States, and I can give my kids education. When I come to the United States. I work very hard. I have a candy store. I work very hard. Only I tried to give my kids the best education.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sent them to Chicago to Northwestern College. OK, we will talk about that afterwards. I just want to continue with before the war. And you said you went to cheder? What ages were you at cheder? At what age did you go to cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go till I was 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till you were 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to the cheder. And then later, I was helping out in the store. And the two sisters, they went to public school. And later, they got one job and the other job. And I was with my mother in the store till the war-- till the war broke out. And when the war broke out, is-- my two sisters and I went to [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you start-- tell me what happened at the start of the war? How did the war start for you? Where were you when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: When--

speaker: interviewer
text: When the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when the war started, I was in the town, in Krasnystaw. This was Friday, September 1. The radio announced that German start a war with Poland. Oh, in the time when the war, we hear the-- the German-- they're knocking out Poland all over. Like-- after two days, they'd send up airplanes like to Lublin-- to the bigger towns. And they bombed the towns. And a lot of people was running out from the towns. They went to the Russian border. There was a lot of people from all over, because it was the closest place to go to the border-- in the Russian. They have a big border with Russia. Well, the-- the-- till the 15th of the-- of September, the-- the German come in in our town. Just like the German come in into our town the first day, they took out seven Jews that was hiding in a basement, and they shot them. They said, they killed a German officer-- the Jews killed a German. It was not the truth, only-- In the 17th of September, the Russian come out and defend to Poland. They said they want to help Poland. While the meantime, they have an agreement with the German. when Molotov went with Ribbentrop, they make, in 1939, an agreement. The Russian, if they will go out in the-- against Poland, they will go to the Wisla-- to the Wisla. They will have Ukraine and Poland to the Wisla. And the other part, but the Wisla will belong to the German. It's not too long-- a couple days-- the German was in our town. Later, the German let the Russian come in. The Russian come in. Was the-- the town was already happy. The Russian come in. This was the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this was the-- the 24th of September. The Russian come in in our town. Well, the people was happy. They was-- when the German was, everybody was afraid to go out. Only when the Russian come in, happy. They got-- we have freedom. We back to life for the-- for the people. Till September, I was thinking about-- about three, four days-- days that the Russian will not-- they come in, and the Russian will not stay here. They go back to border where we were the Bug-- the Bug was the border with Ukraine. Is-- the Russians will have Ukraine, and the German will have Poland [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] till the Bug. [DRINKS] I-- I had my brother living in Wlodzimierz. He was thinking, well, the war maybe will take a couple days, and it will blow over. we went-- I went to Wlodzimierz with my two sisters. One sister was-- went to Lodz, and she got a job in some restaurant, and she worked over there. And the other sister was with me, staying with my brother. Only my mother wrote a letter. She's alone, and she'd like my sister to come back home. This was in 1940. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she-- because we was in the Russian side, she got papers, and she went back. It was-- it was taking-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] is in Wlodzimierz, was a lot of people-- refugees-- where they come from different state. Like from Poland, they come over the border. There was a lot of refugees in there. They was living in the temples, and all they-- in empty space, they was living.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was on the Russian side? This was on the Russian side?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian side.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the Russian was living.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
